title: Born to Domestic Servants
summary: Jones, who asks to be recorded as Clarence B. Jones for his mother Benjamin, was born in 1931 in Philadelphia to domestic-servant parents, a maid-and-cook mother and a chauffeur-gardener father who worked for the Lippincott publishing family, and was placed with foster families before his mother decided on a boarding school.

title: The Boarding School for Colored Boys
summary: From about six to fourteen he lived at a Catholic boarding school for indigent colored and Native American boys run by the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament, the only child there with living parents, who visited and left, prompting the other boys to ask why they did not take him home.

title: Stuttering and the Nuns' Grammar
summary: He recalls developing a serious stutter he later overcame, and the rigorous grammar the nuns drilled into him with a knuckle-rapping pointer, an education in Latin and diagrammed sentences so thorough that decades later an editor marveled at his flawless prose.

title: Not Anti-White, and Most Likely to Succeed
summary: Jones explains to Dr. King years later that the unconditional love and discipline he received as a Negro boy from white Irish nuns left him able to see racism as the ideology of white supremacy without hating white people, and recounts excelling at Palmyra High, voted most likely to succeed.

title: The Navy, Columbia, and Two Nobel Teachers
summary: After he tried to enlist underage in the Navy's college program, his principal and teachers paid his application fees, and at Columbia, on a financial-aid package that demanded a B-minus average, he faced his academic Waterloo in physics under Nobel laureates Linus Pauling and Polykarp Kusch.

title: Working His Way Through, and the Clarinet
summary: Jones describes waiting tables for food and money, his mother surprising him at the Nedick's counter, and the confidence his music gave him as a first-chair clarinetist in the New Jersey all-state orchestra who studied at Juilliard and turned toward jazz.

title: Sitting In With Charlie Parker
summary: At Manny's Music shop the owner sent the shy young clarinetist upstairs to Charlie Parker, who made him assemble his horn and play along through Indiana and On the Sunny Side of the Street, then told him he was on his way, a profound encounter with a genius who improvised within the melodic line.

title: An Emergency Call on His Birthday
summary: Taking midterms on his birthday, Jones was called to the dean's office and learned that his mother had been operated on for rectal cancer, given a colostomy and three or four months to live, leaving his father in inconsolable grief.

title: A Desk at the Foot of Her Bed
summary: Refusing to drop out, he arranged with the dean to keep up his classes and set a desk at the foot of his mother's bed to do his homework, leaving periodically for New York, while she wept and insisted he return to school, not believing he truly had permission to study.

title: The Letter Under the Mattress
summary: After his mother died, wasted to under a hundred pounds, the movers found the letter she had hidden under the mattress: an apology for leaving him, a recipe for the crullers and floating island she could no longer make, and her certainty that he would one day be a great man.

title: Made Fearless, and Drafted
summary: Jones marks his mother's death as the apex of pain that made him fearless, a tempering by the hottest fire, just as the loss of his student deferment after her death led his draft board to take him in August 1953.

title: Paul Robeson's Touchdown and Defiance
summary: He recounts the two encounters with Paul Robeson that shaped him: Robeson telling him one touchdown by a Negro before fifty thousand at Baker Field would do more for equality than any leaflet, and Robeson defying the House committee chaired by James Eastland as unconstitutionally seated.

title: The Statement He Wrote the Night Before
summary: Angry and politicized, and convinced his Mount Holly board had drafted him out of prejudice, Jones turned down a friend's offer to help him flee to Eastern Europe and instead wrote a handwritten statement that he would report for induction but not serve a country that denied him first-class citizenship.

title: Reporting Without Raising His Hand
summary: At Whitehall Street he refused to raise his hand or sign anything, handed officers his statement, and was sent to Fort Dix, where he laughed at being slotted for special-forces training to parachute into the Soviet bloc, wondering how he would explain his Black face in the Russian countryside.

title: Left-Wing Friends and Lorraine Hansberry
summary: Jones traces the New York leftist circle that shaped him, the poet Robert Nemiroff, whose family ran a restaurant where Jones picked up conversational Yiddish and who married the playwright Lorraine Hansberry, and the D'Lugoff brothers of the Village Gate.

title: The Informant Who Confessed
summary: In a newly integrated army he became a target after refusing to sign the loyalty oath, was warned he might be taught a lesson, and was betrayed by a close friend named Hinton who broke down drunk and confessed that his job in the platoon was to write reports on Jones, then vanished.

title: Soldier of the Month, Tried as a Security Risk
summary: Though named soldier of the month and given a good-conduct medal, Jones was charged after twenty-one months as a security risk on absurd allegations, membership in the Communist Party since age seven among them, and was found an undesirable, discharged under armed guard.

title: The Appeal and the Honorable Discharge
summary: With the ACLU lawyer Stanley Faulkner he appealed to the Armed Services Discharge Review Board, won a general then, through a writ of mandamus, an honorable discharge with back pay and GI Bill eligibility, an ordeal he frames as proof that his mother's death had made him fearless.

title: Meeting Ann Norton at the Library
summary: At the New York Public Library Jones ran into Ann Norton, a progressive white woman he had known from Sarah Lawrence parties, who helped type his appeal transcript, became romantically involved with him, and married him in a major turning point of his life.

title: The Banker Father-in-Law and the Clarinet
summary: Jones describes Ann's family behind the Norton publishing house and her stepfather Daniel Crena de Iongh, a Dutch banker and early World Bank treasurer whose closest friend was the apartheid architect Hendrik Verwoerd, and how playing the Mozart clarinet concerto won over his classical-musician mother-in-law.

title: Idealism, Class, and Apartheid at the Table
summary: He paints Ann's deep idealism alongside an upper-class certainty that could look down on others, her friendship with the Dole pineapple heiress Damon, and her rebellion against a stepfather she could not reconcile with what he did, which she felt Jones treated too gently.

title: The Boston Apartment and the Irish Superintendent
summary: After apartments fell through once landlords learned they were an interracial couple, Ann secured a Beacon Street place sight-unseen for her husband, and Jones won over the astonished Irish superintendent with his boarding-school memories of Irish nuns and Danny Boy, gaining a devoted friend.

part: Law, Hollywood, and Meeting Dr. King
title: Copyright Law and Entertainment in California
summary: Jones recounts his prize-winning copyright paper at Boston University, his professor Donald Shapiro's counsel that the real power in entertainment is behind the camera, and his move to California to work in entertainment law until his mentor at Revue Productions died of a heart attack.

title: Judge Delany's Call and a Refusal
summary: Judge Hubert Delany asked Jones to come south as a law clerk on Dr. King's Alabama tax-perjury defense, and when Jones declined for practical reasons, King himself flew to California and came to his Altadena home to ask for the help of a young Negro lawyer, which Jones again put off.

title: The Sermon That Changed His Mind
summary: Invited to King's guest sermon, Jones heard the preacher describe him from the pulpit and recast Langston Hughes's Mother to Son with Jones's own mother as the actor, moving him to tears and to walk up afterward and ask, Dr. King, when do you want me to go to Montgomery.

title: Birmingham's Spark and Lending King the House
summary: Jones frames Birmingham as the spark that lit thirteen hundred demonstrations across the country, and recounts how, with King exhausted, Stanley Levison and Bayard Rustin arranged for the Joneses to vacate their new home so King could vacation there and stay reachable for the March.

title: The Pressure on Levison and the Speaker Fight
summary: He recounts President Kennedy pressing King to cut ties with Stanley Levison and Jack O'Dell over alleged Communist ties, and the March committee meeting where conservative clergy maneuvered to limit each speaker and bury King in the middle of the program.

title: Opening Act or Major Act
summary: Standing in for the absent King, Jones drew on his concert-business knowledge to ask the committee whether they really wanted to follow Dr. King, an opening act before the major act, and with A. Philip Randolph's backing, King was made the final speaker.

title: The Promissory Note, and Mahalia's Shout
summary: Jones describes drafting the speech's opening, the promissory note and the bank of justice with insufficient funds, which King delivered verbatim, until Mahalia Jackson called out to tell them about the dream and King set the text aside to speak extemporaneously.

title: A Cosmic Force, and the Genius of Nonviolence
summary: Jones testifies that he had never heard King speak that way before or after, as if a cosmic force took over his body, and lays out the political genius of summoning the conscience of the 88 percent rather than imposing the case of the 12 percent by force.

title: A Moral Compass for America
summary: He argues that King made segregation a moral rather than a legal question, planting a moral compass in Birmingham and taking an addicted America by the hand toward a redemptive, nonviolent recovery, insisting that one day the descendants of slaves and slaveholders would sit at the table of brotherhood.

title: From the Dream to Today's Violence
summary: Jones turns King's legacy on the present, defending LGBT people against the conservative Black church, tying the 1963 images of Birmingham's fire hoses to the children killed at Newtown, and refusing to commemorate the March without confronting the ubiquitous violence in the country.

title: The Broken Family and the Omega Boys Club
summary: He points to the destruction of family structure and the 1980 arrival of crack cocaine as fundamental changes, and to Dr. Joseph Marshall's Omega Boys Club as a substitute parental discipline that keeps young people alive and free, with King's reminder that a teacher must tell a story.

title: Wealth, War, and What King Would Say
summary: Jones closes on the parts of King's legacy he says must be honored, the fight against wealth disparity behind the Memphis sanitation strike and echoed by Occupy Wall Street, the moral and practical opposition to the Vietnam War, and his reading that King would meet even same-sex marriage with love, the lion telling his own story so the hunters do not get all the credit.
